title: Von dem Mäuschen, Vögelchen und der Bratwurst
titleEn: The Mouse, the Bird, and the Sausage
part: The Mouse, the Bird, and the Sausage
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 23. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The twenty-third tale in the Grimms' collection (KHM 23), a comic household fable. A mouse, a bird, and a sausage keep house together, each with its own task, until the bird is talked into thinking the division unfair and insists they swap roles; disaster follows at once — a dog devours the sausage on its way to the woods, the mouse perishes in the pot she tries to grease as the sausage once did, and the bird, hurrying for water, drops the bucket into the well and drowns with it. It is a small parable on discontent: whoever is too well off hankers after new things. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gerathen, müßte, daß, Loos, Gemüs, thun and gehöret.

sentences.t: Es waren einmal ein Mäuschen, ein Vögelchen und eine Bratwurst in Gesellschaft gerathen, hatten einen Haushalt geführt, lange wohl und köstlich im Frieden gelebt, und trefflich an Gütern zugenommen.
sentences.l: There had once a little-mouse, a little-bird and a sausage fallen into company together, had kept a household, long lived well and daintily in peace, and had excellently increased in goods.
sentences.i: Once upon a time a little mouse, a little bird, and a sausage had joined in fellowship together; they had set up house, long lived well and daintily in peace, and had prospered handsomely in worldly goods.
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten; “in Gesellschaft gerathen” = to have fallen in together, joined company. “zugenommen” (zunehmen, to increase) is a separable verb — “an Gütern zunehmen” = to grow in wealth. Note the fond diminutives Mäuschen and Vögelchen (‘little mouse,’ ‘little bird’), which run through the whole tale.

sentences.t: Des Vögelchens Arbeit war, daß es täglich im Wald fliegen und Holz beibringen müßte.
sentences.l: The little-bird's work was that it daily had to fly in the wood and bring in wood.
sentences.i: The little bird's task was that every day it had to fly into the forest and fetch firewood.
sentences.n: “Des Vögelchens” is genitive, ‘the little bird's.’ “beibringen” here has its older sense ‘to bring in, supply’ (the firewood) — in modern German it usually means ‘to teach.’ “müßte” is subjunctive (and carries the 1857 ß); “daß” is likewise the old spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Die Maus sollte Wasser tragen, Feuer anmachen und den Tisch decken, die Bratwurst aber sollte kochen.
sentences.l: The mouse should carry water, make fire and set the table, but the sausage should cook.
sentences.i: The mouse was to carry water, light the fire and lay the table, but the sausage was to do the cooking.
sentences.n: The three-way division of labour that the whole fable turns on. “Feuer anmachen” (anmachen, to light) is a separable verb; “den Tisch decken” = to lay the table. “sollte” here marks assigned duty, ‘was to.’

sentences.t: Wem zu wohl ist, den gelüstet immer nach neuen Dingen!
sentences.l: Whom it is too well with, him it always craves after new things!
sentences.i: Whoever is too well off always hankers after new things!
sentences.n: The proverb at the heart of the tale — a warning against discontent. It is doubly impersonal: dative “wem” (‘the one for whom [things are too good]’) and accusative “den” as the object of impersonal “gelüsten” (‘it craves someone after something’). Keep this line in mind; the plot is its illustration.

sentences.t: Also eines Tages stieß dem Vöglein unterwegs ein anderer Vogel auf, dem es seine treffliche Gelegenheit erzählte und rühmte.
sentences.l: So one day another bird came upon the little-bird on the way, to whom it told and praised its excellent situation.
sentences.i: So one day, on the road, the little bird fell in with another bird, to whom it told and boasted of its splendid arrangement.
sentences.n: “stieß ... auf” (aufstoßen, here ‘to chance upon, meet’) is separable and takes the dative “dem Vöglein.” “Gelegenheit” has its older sense of ‘situation, circumstances,’ not ‘opportunity.’ “rühmen” = to praise, extol, boast of. Note “Vöglein,” another diminutive of Vogel used interchangeably with Vögelchen.

sentences.t: Derselbe andere Vogel schalt es aber einen armen Tropf, der große Arbeit, die beiden zu Haus aber gute Tage hätten.
sentences.l: But that same other bird scolded it as a poor simpleton — the great work [being its own], while the two at home had good days.
sentences.i: But that same other bird chided it for a poor fool: all the hard work was its own, while the two at home led an easy life.
sentences.n: “schalt” is the preterite of schelten (to scold, chide); “einen armen Tropf” = a poor wretch, a simpleton. The sentence is elliptical — a verb is dropped in the middle clause. “hätten” is the subjunctive of reported speech (the other bird's claim, not stated fact), a pattern you meet constantly in Grimm.

sentences.t: Denn, wenn die Maus ihr Feuer angemacht und Wasser getragen hatte, so begab sie sich in ihr Kämmerlein zur Ruhe bis man sie hieß den Tisch decken.
sentences.l: For, when the mouse had made her fire and carried water, then she betook herself into her little-chamber to rest, until one bade her lay the table.
sentences.i: For once the mouse had lit her fire and carried the water, she would betake herself to her little chamber to rest, until she was told to lay the table.
sentences.n: The old resumptive “wenn ..., so ...” (‘when ..., then ...’) sets the rhythm. “sich begeben” = to betake oneself, go. “Kämmerlein” is the diminutive of Kammer (‘little chamber’). “hieß” here is heißen in the sense ‘bade, ordered’ — “man hieß sie den Tisch decken” = they told her to lay the table.

sentences.t: Das Würstlein blieb beim Hafen, sah zu daß die Speise wohl kochte, und wenn es bald Essenszeit war, schlingte es sich ein mal viere durch den Brei oder das Gemüs, so war es geschmalzen, gesalzen und bereitet.
sentences.l: The little-sausage stayed by the pot, saw to it that the food cooked well, and when it was soon meal-time, it wound itself a time or four through the porridge or the vegetables, and so it was greased, salted and made ready.
sentences.i: The little sausage stayed by the pot, saw that the food cooked well, and when mealtime drew near, it would wind itself a time or four through the porridge or the vegetables, and so the dish was greased, salted and ready.
sentences.n: This is the sausage's peculiar task: it seasons the pot by swimming through the food, its own fat greasing the dish — hence “geschmalzen” (from Schmalz, fat/lard). “Hafen” here is an old word for a cooking pot, not ‘harbour.’ “schlingte” is an old weak preterite of schlingen (to wind, coil), here of slithering through; “ein mal viere” is a folksy ‘a time or four,’ i.e. a few times. “Gemüs” is the 1857 form of Gemüse.

sentences.t: Kam dann das Vöglein heim und legte seine Bürde ab, so saßen sie zu Tisch, und nach gehabtem Mahl schliefen sie sich die Haut voll bis den andern Morgen; und das war ein herrlich Leben.
sentences.l: Then when the little-bird came home and laid down its burden, they sat down to table, and after the meal they had, they slept themselves skin-full until the next morning; and that was a glorious life.
sentences.i: Then when the little bird came home and set down its load, they would sit down to table, and after the meal was done they slept their fill until the next morning; and that was a glorious life.
sentences.n: “kam ... heim” (heimkommen) and “legte ... ab” (ablegen) are both separable. “nach gehabtem Mahl” = after the meal was had (the participle gehabt used adjectivally, a formal turn). “sich die Haut voll schlafen” — literally ‘to sleep the skin full,’ a hearty folk idiom for sleeping one's fill.

sentences.t: Das Vöglein anderes Tages wollte aus Anstiftung nicht mehr ins Holz, sprechend es wäre lang genug Knecht gewesen, und hätte gleichsam ihr Narr sein müssen, sie sollten einmal umwechseln und es auf eine andere Weise auch versuchen.
sentences.l: The little-bird, the next day, out of instigation wanted no more into the wood, saying it had been servant long enough, and had, as it were, had to be their fool; they should for once change about and try it also in another way.
sentences.i: The next day the little bird, egged on, would go into the woods no more, saying it had been a drudge long enough and had been, as it were, their fool; they ought to swap round for once and try it another way too.
sentences.n: “aus Anstiftung” = at [the other bird's] instigation, egged on. “sprechend” is a present participle, ‘saying’ — an elevated, archaic construction. The reported complaint runs in the subjunctive: “wäre ... gewesen ... hätte ... sein müssen.” “Knecht” = farmhand, drudge; “gleichsam” = as it were; “ins Holz” = into the woods (Holz means both firewood and woodland).

sentences.t: Und wie wohl die Maus und auch die Bratwurst heftig dafür bat, so war der Vogel doch Meister: es mußte gewagt sein, spieleten derowegen, und kam das Loos auf die Bratwurst, die mußte Holz tragen, die Maus ward Koch, und der Vogel sollte Wasser holen.
sentences.l: And however much the mouse and the sausage too pleaded hard about it, the bird was master after all: it had to be ventured; they therefore cast lots, and the lot came upon the sausage, which had to carry wood, the mouse became cook, and the bird should fetch water.
sentences.i: And for all the mouse and the sausage pleaded hard about it, the bird had its way after all: the thing had to be risked. So they drew lots, and the lot fell to the sausage, who now had to carry the wood; the mouse became cook, and the bird was to fetch water.
sentences.n: “wie wohl” = wiewohl, ‘although.’ “der Vogel war doch Meister” = the bird was master/had its way after all. “es mußte gewagt sein” = it had to be risked. “spieleten ... das Loos” — an old preterite of spielen, here ‘they cast lots’; “derowegen” is archaic for ‘therefore’ (= deswegen), and “Loos” the 1857 spelling of Los (lot). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden.

sentences.t: Was geschieht?
sentences.l: What happens?
sentences.n: A lively narrator's aside: the tense jumps to the present (“geschieht,” “findet” below) in the middle of a past-tense story, drawing the reader in — ‘and what happens next?’

sentences.t: das Bratwürstchen zog fort gen Holz, das Vöglein machte Feuer an, die Maus stellte den Topf zu, und erwarteten allein, bis Bratwürstchen heim käme und Holz für den andern Tag brächte.
sentences.l: the little-sausage set off toward the wood, the little-bird made the fire, the mouse set the pot on, and they only waited until the little-sausage should come home and bring wood for the next day.
sentences.i: the little sausage set off toward the woods, the little bird lit the fire, the mouse set the pot on, and they only waited for the little sausage to come home and bring wood for the next day.
sentences.n: “gen” is archaic for ‘toward, against’ (= gegen), as in “gen Himmel.” “zog fort” (fortziehen), “machte ... an” (anmachen) and “stellte ... zu” (zustellen) are all separable verbs. “käme” and “brächte” are subjunctives of expectation. The same sausage is now Bratwürstchen, now Würstlein — diminutives varied for the ear.

sentences.t: Es blieb aber das Würstlein so lang unterwegs, daß ihnen beiden nichts gutes vorkam, und das Vöglein ein Stück Luft hinaus entgegen flog.
sentences.l: But the little-sausage stayed on the way so long that to them both nothing good seemed [to bode], and the little-bird flew out a stretch of air to meet it.
sentences.i: But the little sausage stayed away on the road so long that the two of them had a foreboding, and the little bird flew out a stretch of the way to meet it.
sentences.n: “vorkommen” here means ‘to seem, occur’ — “es kam ihnen nichts Gutes vor” = they sensed something was amiss. “ein Stück Luft hinaus” is a quaint turn, ‘a piece of air outward,’ i.e. a stretch of the way through the air. “entgegen flog” (entgegenfliegen, to fly to meet) is separable.

sentences.t: Unfern aber findet es einen Hund am Weg, der das arme Bratwürstlein als freie Beut angetroffen, angepackt und niedergemacht.
sentences.l: But not far off it finds a dog by the way, who had come upon the poor little-sausage as free prey, seized it and done it in.
sentences.i: But not far off it comes upon a dog by the roadside, who had found the poor little sausage as fair game, set upon it, and made an end of it.
sentences.n: “Unfern” = not far off (elevated); the present tense “findet” again quickens the pace. “freie Beut” (= freie Beute) = fair game, prey for the taking; “Beut” is an old shortened form. The three participles “angetroffen, angepackt und niedergemacht” share an understood ‘hatte’; “niedermachen” = to kill, do in.

sentences.t: Das Vöglein beschwerte sich auch dessen als eines offenbaren Raubes sehr gegen den Hund, aber es half kein Wort, denn, sprach der Hund, er hätte falsche Briefe bei der Bratwurst gefunden, deswegen wäre sie ihm des Lebens verfallen gewesen.
sentences.l: The little-bird also complained of this, as of a manifest robbery, greatly against the dog, but no word helped, for, said the dog, he had found false letters on the sausage, therefore it had forfeited its life to him.
sentences.i: The little bird protested loudly to the dog against this too, as an open robbery, but not a word availed; for, said the dog, he had found forged papers on the sausage, and for that its life was forfeit to him.
sentences.n: “sich beschweren” takes the genitive of the thing complained of — “beschwerte sich dessen ... als eines offenbaren Raubes” (‘of this, as a manifest robbery’). “es half kein Wort” = not a word availed. The dog's excuse — “falsche Briefe” (forged papers) — is a mock-legal absurdity. “des Lebens verfallen” = forfeit of one's life (verfallen + genitive); the reported claim stands in the subjunctive “hätte ... wäre ... verfallen gewesen.”

sentences.t: Das Vöglein, traurig, nahm das Holz auf sich, flog heim und erzählte was es gesehn und gehöret.
sentences.l: The little-bird, sad, took the wood upon itself, flew home and told what it had seen and heard.
sentences.i: The little bird, sorrowful, took the wood upon itself, flew home, and told what it had seen and heard.
sentences.n: “nahm ... auf sich” = took upon itself, shouldered. “gesehn” and “gehöret” are older spellings of the participles gesehen and gehört.

sentences.t: Sie waren sehr betrübt, verglichen sich aber das beste zu thun und beisammen zu bleiben.
sentences.l: They were very grieved, but agreed with one another to do the best and to stay together.
sentences.i: They were much grieved, but agreed to make the best of it and to stay together.
sentences.n: “sich vergleichen” here carries its old sense ‘to come to an agreement, settle,’ not the modern ‘to compare.’ “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (with the old th-). “beisammen” = together.

sentences.t: Derowegen so deckte das Vöglein den Tisch und die Maus rüstete das Essen, und wollte anrichten, und in den Hafen, wie zuvor das Würstlein, durch das Gemüs schlingen und schlupfen, dasselbe zu schmelzen: aber ehe sie in die Mitte kam, ward sie angehalten und mußte Haut und Haar und dabei das Leben lassen.
sentences.l: Therefore the little-bird set the table and the mouse made ready the meal, and wanted to dish up, and into the pot, as the little-sausage before, to wind and slip through the vegetables, so as to grease it: but before she came to the middle, she was held fast and had to leave skin and hair and thereby her life.
sentences.i: So the little bird laid the table and the mouse got the meal ready, and meant to dish it up, and — into the pot, as the sausage had done before — to wind and slip through the vegetables to grease it: but before she reached the middle, she was caught fast and had to give up skin and hair, and with them her life.
sentences.n: The mouse takes on the sausage's fatal task and it kills her. “Derowegen so” doubly marks ‘therefore’ (archaic). “anrichten” = to dish up, serve; “Hafen” again = pot. “schlingen und schlupfen” = to wind and slip (schlupfen an old form of schlüpfen); “schmelzen” here = to render fat, grease (the dish). “ward ... angehalten” = was stopped/caught; “Haut und Haar ... lassen” = to lose everything, skin and hair — here literally her life.

sentences.t: Als das Vöglein kam und wollte das Essen auftragen, da war kein Koch vorhanden.
sentences.l: When the little-bird came and wanted to serve up the food, there was no cook at hand.
sentences.i: When the little bird came and wanted to serve the meal, there was no cook to be found.
sentences.n: “auftragen” (to serve up, bring to table) is separable. “vorhanden” = at hand, present, to be found.

sentences.t: Das Vöglein warf bestürzt das Holz hin und her, rufte und suchte, konnte aber seinen Koch nicht mehr finden.
sentences.l: The little-bird, dismayed, threw the wood this way and that, called and searched, but could no longer find its cook.
sentences.i: The little bird, dismayed, threw the wood hither and thither, called and searched, but could no longer find its cook.
sentences.n: “bestürzt” = dismayed, aghast. “hin und her” = to and fro, this way and that. “rufte” is an older weak preterite of rufen (modern rief). “nicht mehr” = no longer.

sentences.t: Aus Unachtsamkeit kam das Feuer in das Holz, also daß eine Brunst entstand; das Vöglein eilte Wasser zu langen, da entfiel ihm der Eimer in den Brunnen, und es mit hinab, daß es sich nicht mehr erholen konnte und da ersaufen mußte.
sentences.l: Out of carelessness the fire got into the wood, so that a blaze arose; the little-bird hurried to fetch water, then the bucket fell from it into the well, and it along down [with it], so that it could no longer recover itself and had to drown there.
sentences.i: Through carelessness the fire caught the wood, so that a blaze broke out; the little bird hurried to draw water, but the bucket slipped from its grasp into the well, and it fell in after, so that it could not save itself and had to drown there.
sentences.n: “aus Unachtsamkeit” = out of carelessness. “also daß” = so that (result). “Brunst” here means blaze, conflagration (an old sense; in modern German it is ‘rut, heat’). “langen” = to reach, fetch. “entfiel ihm der Eimer” = the bucket fell from him (dative ihm of loss); “und es mit hinab” drops its verb (fell) — ‘and it down along with it.’ “ersaufen” is the coarse word for drowning used of animals — the same low register Grimm uses for the wolf in the Geislein tale.
